text: Hello friends, today I am going to demonstrate robotic transital esophagectomy for

seventy two year gentleman with lower thoracic squamous cell carcinoma who have received neoadjuvant chemo radiotherapy patient is having poor lung conditions he is old and fragile and hence trans-hiatal oesophagectomy was planned abdominal part was done as usual i will start with the trans-hiatal dissection video is two point five x our first aim is to create a surgical field

The surgeon retracts the hiatus anteriorly and to the right, exposing the station 111 lymph node, which is removed with the specimen. This action reveals a clear plane of dissection between the specimen and the pericardium.
The surgeon removes all fibro-fatty tissue along with the esophageal specimen, ensuring a clear dissection plane.
A clear dissection plane is visible between the esophagectomy specimen and the mediastinal pleura.
The surgeon clears the surgical field by dissecting the mesoesophagus from both the left and right sides of the mediastinal pleura, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the esophagectomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection anteriorly after creating adequate space, focusing on separating the esophageal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection posteriorly, continuing to separate the specimen from the surrounding structures.
The mesoesophagus is lifted anteriorly, revealing a clear plane between the mesoesophagus and the descending aorta.
The surgeon demonstrates a clear avascular plane between the specimen and the mediastinal pleura, highlighting the separation achieved during dissection.
Trans-ital esophagectomy is usually taken as a blind procedure and oncological inferior as no mediastinal lymph node dissection is attempted in trans-ital esophagectomy.

But with robotic technology, it is no more a blind procedure and standard two-field lymph node dissection which is also known as infra-oxaic mediastinal lymph node dissection can be achieved with robotic trans-ital esophagectomy.
The video shows three distinct surgical fields created during the esophagectomy procedure, allowing for targeted dissection and manipulation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon manipulates the right anterolateral aspect of the surgical field, focusing on the area between the mesoesophagus and the descending aorta.
The surgeon dissects and removes tissue from the right side of the periosteovisceral area, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the left anterolateral and posterior surgical fields, which are part of the three distinct fields created for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon delicately addresses all three surgical fields, excising the respective lymph node basins and including them with the esophagectomy specimen.
The surgeon separates the mesoesophagus along with the perisophageal tissue from the left side of the mediastinal pleura, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the esophagectomy procedure.
The descending aorta is visible in the surgical field, surrounded by perisophageal tissue and mediastinal structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus and surrounding tissue, progressing towards the arch of the aorta, with visible separation of the mesoesophagus and perisophageal tissue from the mediastinal pleura.
It is extremely important to keep the surgical field very dry so that we can demonstrate all the important anatomical landmarks.
The surgical field reveals the pericardium positioned anteriorly and the descending aorta located posteriorly, providing clear anatomical landmarks during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the mesoesophageal tissue and lymph nodes along with the esophagus specimen, ensuring all surrounding tissue is included in the resection.
The surgeon approaches the arch of the aorta, carefully dissecting and removing branches of the left vagus nerve that obstruct the surgical path.
The surgeon identifies the autopulmonary window, a key anatomical landmark on the left side, visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field displays the autopulmonary window containing soft tissue and lymph nodes, which are part of the esophageal specimen being dissected.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection with monopolar scissors to separate the tissue from the left side mediastinal pleura, the downward arch of the aorta, and anteriorly from the main pulmonary trunk.

The surgeon cuts the main trunk of the left vagus nerve, releasing the specimen, and repeats similar steps on the right side by defining and cutting the right vagus nerve. The dissection proceeds to the vicinity of the right station 109 lymph node, where the entire lymph node basin is taken with the specimen.
The surgeon carefully defines the right bronchus and proceeds to remove the station 107 subcarinal lymph node, ensuring no bleeding occurs during the dissection.

The surgical field is kept clear to avoid messiness as the surgeon proceeds cranially after removing station 107 lymph node. The right bronchial artery is visible anteriorly, serving as a landmark to define the right bronchus. The dissection is performed flush to the esophagus.
The surgeon performs lymph node clearance in the region of the azygos vein, removing lymphatic tissue as part of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon approaches station 109 lymph node, the final lymph node station to be dissected before proceeding to the neck dissection.
The surgeon identifies the inferior border of the left bronchus beneath the station 109 lymph node.
This is the anatomy of the transital esophagectomy.
The surgeon removes all mesoesophageal tissue along with the specimen, ensuring complete excision of the targeted area.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, revealing the azygos vein and thoracic duct running parallel on the right side of the surgical field. The descending aorta is visible on the left side, ensuring these structures remain within view to minimize the risk of injury.

The surgeon identifies an avascular plane between the prevascular fascia covering major vessels and the visceral fascia covering the specimen, leading up to the thoracic inlet. The thoracic duct is visible as an important landmark embedded within the posterior fat, marking the conclusion of the transital part of the robotic transital esophagectomy.
The surgeon creates a pneumoperitoneum in the neck plane, expanding the surgical field for further dissection.
Thank you very much.
